transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Lily. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling really overwhelmed lately. There’s this situation with my friend Ethan that’s been weighing on me. I just feel like I should be doing more to help him, but I can’t seem to reach him. It’s hard to shake off this feeling that I’m failing as a friend.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy weight with the situation involving Ethan. Feeling overwhelmed and like you’re not doing enough can be really tough. Can you tell me a bit more about what’s been going on with him and how you’ve been feeling about it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... I’ve been trying to get in touch with him for a while now, but he hasn’t been responding. I keep thinking that if I just try harder or reach out more, maybe I can help him. But it feels like I’m just stuck. I feel anxious and worried all the time, like I’m not doing enough. It’s hard to focus on anything else when I’m so preoccupied with this. I don’t know... it just makes me feel really sad and lonely.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of anxiety and sadness around your friendship with Ethan, especially since you’re worried about not being able to reach him. That sense of being stuck and preoccupied must be really draining for you. When you think about trying harder to help him, what kind of thoughts come to mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I keep thinking that it’s my responsibility to fix things for him. Like, if I don’t do something, then I’m failing as a friend. It’s like this pressure I put on myself. I know I shouldn’t feel that way, but it’s hard to shake off. I just feel like if I can’t help him, then what kind of friend am I? It’s frustrating because I want to be there for him, but I feel so helpless.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like you’re carrying a heavy sense of responsibility for Ethan’s well-being, which is creating a lot of pressure and frustration for you. It’s understandable to want to be there for a friend, but it sounds like this pressure is making you feel helpless instead. When you think about what it means to be a good friend, what beliefs or expectations come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I believe that a good friend should always be there for each other, no matter what. If someone is struggling, I feel like it’s my job to help them through it. I think I’ve always thought that being supportive means being available and solving their problems. But now, with Ethan, it feels like I’m failing at that, and it’s hard to reconcile that with what I think a good friend should be. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of expectations that I can’t meet, and it just makes everything feel heavier.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a strong belief about friendship being tied to always being available and solving problems, which is creating a lot of internal conflict for you right now. That loop of expectations can be really exhausting, especially when you feel like you’re not meeting them. I can see how that would make you feel even more overwhelmed and sad. Have you had experiences in the past that shaped this belief about what it means to be a good friend?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not really sure. I think I’ve always been the one in my friend group who tries to help others. Maybe it comes from wanting to be there for people the way I wish someone had been there for me at times. I just... I guess I’ve internalized this idea that if I’m not helping, then I’m not being a good friend. It’s hard to think about it too much because it makes me feel vulnerable. I just want to do the right thing, you know? But I don’t know if I really know what that is anymore.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your desire to help others comes from a deep place of wanting to provide support, perhaps because you’ve felt a lack of it at times yourself. That’s a really compassionate motivation, but it also seems to have created a lot of pressure for you. Feeling vulnerable when reflecting on this is completely understandable. It’s tough to navigate what it means to do the right thing when your own beliefs and feelings are in conflict. What do you think it would look like to be a good friend to Ethan, even if it doesn’t align perfectly with those expectations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think... maybe being a good friend could also mean just being there for him in a different way. Like, instead of trying to fix everything, I could just let him know I care and that I’m here for him, even if I can’t reach him right now. It’s hard to let go of that need to solve things, but I guess sometimes just being present is enough. I don’t know if I fully believe that yet, but it feels like it could be a step in the right direction. It’s just... letting go of that pressure feels really daunting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s a significant realization that being a good friend can also mean simply being present and showing you care, even if you can’t solve everything. It’s understandable that letting go of that pressure feels daunting, especially when it’s been such a strong part of how you view friendship. It seems like you’re beginning to explore a different perspective, which is a positive step. What do you think might help you take that step toward letting go of the pressure, even just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe talking about it more with friends could help. Sharing how I’m feeling and getting their perspectives might ease some of that pressure. I also wonder if I could set some boundaries for myself, like limiting how much I dwell on the situation with Ethan. It’s just hard to break that cycle of feeling responsible. I guess finding ways to remind myself that I can care without having to fix everything could be a start. It’s just... I’m not sure how to do that yet.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re considering some helpful strategies, like talking to your friends about how you’re feeling and setting boundaries for yourself. Those could be great ways to ease the pressure and gain some perspective. It’s completely normal to feel uncertain about how to shift your mindset, especially when it’s been so ingrained. What do you think would be the first small step you could take toward reminding yourself that caring doesn’t always mean fixing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by writing down my thoughts and feelings about the situation. Just getting everything out on paper might help me see things more clearly. I could also try to remind myself that it’s okay to not have all the answers. I could even send Ethan a message just letting him know I’m thinking of him, without any pressure to respond or fix anything. It feels like a small step, but I think it might help me feel a little less burdened. I just hope it doesn’t feel too awkward.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Writing down your thoughts and feelings sounds like a great way to process everything and gain clarity. It’s also a gentle approach to reaching out to Ethan without the pressure of needing a response or a solution. It’s completely normal to feel a bit awkward about it, but taking those small steps can be really meaningful. It seems like you’re finding ways to care for both Ethan and yourself, which is important. How do you feel about giving yourself permission to take those small steps, even if they feel a bit uncomfortable?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think giving myself permission feels... a little freeing, actually. It’s like I’m acknowledging that it’s okay to not have everything figured out and that I can still care without the weight of responsibility. It’s still a bit scary, but I guess it’s also a chance to take a little pressure off myself. I want to try to be kinder to myself in this process. I’m realizing that it’s okay to feel uncomfortable while I figure things out. I just hope I can stick to it and not fall back into that old mindset of needing to fix everything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that giving yourself permission feels freeing and that you’re recognizing the importance of being kinder to yourself during this process. Acknowledging that discomfort is part of growth is a significant insight. It’s natural to have moments where you might slip back into old patterns, but it sounds like you’re building a foundation for a more compassionate approach to yourself and your friendship with Ethan. What do you think might help you stay mindful of this new perspective as you move forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe setting reminders for myself could help. Like, I could write down some affirmations or notes about what I’ve learned and keep them somewhere visible. It might also be helpful to check in with friends regularly, so I have support and encouragement. I could even create a little mantra for myself, something like “Caring doesn’t mean fixing.” Just having those reminders could help me stay grounded when I start to feel overwhelmed again. I want to keep this perspective in mind, even when things get tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like really practical and supportive strategies! Having visible reminders and affirmations can be a powerful way to reinforce your new perspective. Checking in with friends for support is also a great way to stay connected and feel less alone in this process. Creating a mantra like “Caring doesn’t mean fixing” could serve as a helpful anchor for you when you start to feel overwhelmed. It seems like you’re taking thoughtful steps to nurture both yourself and your friendship with Ethan. How does it feel to think about these strategies as a way to support yourself moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It feels encouraging, honestly. Just thinking about having those strategies in place makes me feel a bit more hopeful. It’s like I’m giving myself a chance to grow and learn from this experience instead of just feeling stuck. I know it won’t be easy, but having a plan makes it feel more manageable. I want to believe that I can navigate this without being so hard on myself. It’s nice to think that I can care for Ethan while also taking care of myself. That balance feels important.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s heartening to hear that you feel encouraged and hopeful about having a plan in place. Recognizing that you can grow and learn from this experience, while also taking care of yourself, is a significant step toward finding that balance. It’s clear that you’re committed to nurturing both your friendship with Ethan and your own well-being, which is so important. As you move forward, what do you think will be the most important thing to keep in mind to help maintain that balance?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the most important thing to keep in mind is that it’s okay to prioritize my own well-being. I need to remind myself that I can’t pour from an empty cup, and taking care of myself doesn’t mean I care any less about Ethan. It’s about finding that balance and being gentle with myself when I feel overwhelmed. I want to remember that it’s okay to set boundaries and that I don’t have to have all the answers. Just being there for him in whatever way I can is enough. Keeping that perspective will help me stay grounded as I navigate this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful insight, Lily. Prioritizing your own well-being and recognizing that it doesn’t diminish your care for Ethan is a powerful perspective. It’s clear that you’re committed to being gentle with yourself and finding that balance, which will serve you well as you navigate this situation. Keeping those reminders about boundaries and being present without needing to have all the answers will help you stay grounded. As you reflect on this journey, what do you hope to take away from it, both for yourself and your friendship with Ethan?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I hope to take away a deeper understanding of what it means to be a friend, and that it’s okay to not always have the answers or solutions. I want to learn to be more compassionate with myself and to recognize that my worth isn’t tied to how much I can help others. For my friendship with Ethan, I hope to strengthen our connection by being there for him in a way that feels authentic, without the pressure to fix everything. I want to grow from this experience and come out of it feeling more resilient and aware of my own needs, while still being a supportive friend. That balance feels really important to me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are profound takeaways, Lily. Gaining a deeper understanding of friendship and learning to be compassionate with yourself are valuable lessons that can enrich both your life and your relationships. It’s clear that you’re committed to nurturing a more authentic connection with Ethan, free from the pressure to fix things. Your desire to grow and become more resilient while still being a supportive friend speaks to your strength and self-awareness. As you continue on this journey, how do you feel about checking in with yourself periodically to see how you’re doing with these goals?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself periodically sounds really helpful. It would give me a chance to reflect on how I’m feeling and whether I’m staying true to these goals. I could set aside some time each week to think about what’s working and what’s not, and adjust my approach if I need to. It’s a way to hold myself accountable without being too hard on myself. I want to make sure I’m not losing sight of my own needs while trying to support Ethan. I think those check-ins could help me stay balanced and remind me to be kind to myself throughout this process.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful plan, Lily. Setting aside time each week for self-reflection and check-ins can be a great way to stay aligned with your goals and ensure you’re prioritizing your own needs. It’s a gentle approach to accountability that allows you to adjust as needed without being overly critical of yourself. This practice can help reinforce your commitment to balance and self-compassion as you navigate your friendship with Ethan. How do you feel about starting this check-in process? Is there anything specific you’d like to focus on during your first reflection?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel pretty good about starting the check-in process. It seems like a constructive way to keep myself on track. For my first reflection, I think I’d like to focus on how I’m managing my feelings of pressure and responsibility regarding Ethan. I want to see if I can notice when those feelings come up and how I’m responding to them. I’d also like to reflect on any moments where I feel like I’m being a supportive friend without the need to fix everything. Just recognizing those moments could help reinforce this new perspective. I’m looking forward to seeing how it goes!
